title: Non-Qualified Deferred Compensation Plans
topics: Identify NQDC risks and planning considerations
Apply SERP and Top Hat plan concepts

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Non-qualified deferred compensation (NQDC) plans allow executives to defer income beyond qualified plan limits—but with significant risks.

sections.title: The Fundamental Risk
sections.content: NQDC is an UNSECURED PROMISE to pay in the future. You are a general creditor. If employer is insolvent, you stand in line with other creditors and may receive pennies on the dollar—or nothing.

sections.title: Why Employers Use NQDC
items: For Executives: Defer income beyond 401(k) limits, tax benefits if lower future bracket, retention tool
For Employers: No cost until payment, can discriminate (select executives only), "golden handcuffs" retention

sections.title: Section 409A Requirements
rows: Deferral elections before year income is earned
Cannot be secured in offshore trust

sections.title: Six Permitted Distribution Events
items: 1. Separation from service
2. Disability
3. Death
4. Change in ownership/control
5. Unforeseeable emergency
6. Specified date or fixed schedule

sections.title: 409A Violation Penalties
sections.content: If plan fails to comply: Immediate income inclusion of all vested deferrals + Income tax at ordinary rates + 20% additional penalty + Interest on underpayment from deferral year. Severe penalties make 409A compliance critical.

sections.title: Types of NQDC Arrangements
rows: Supplemental Executive Retirement Plan - employer-funded, formula-based
Makes up for qualified plan limits
Unfunded plan for select management/highly compensated

sections.title: Rabbi Trust
sections.content: An irrevocable trust to hold NQDC assets, BUT assets remain subject to employer's creditors in bankruptcy. Provides protection against corporate changes (mergers, management changes), does NOT protect against employer insolvency. Why "Rabbi" Trust? First IRS ruling was for a synagogue rabbi's deferred compensation.

sections.title: When NQDC Makes Sense
rows: Similar current vs. future tax rate

sections.title: Tax Treatment Summary
sections.content: For Employee: Deferral - not taxed when earned; Growth - not taxed until distributed; Distribution - ordinary income when received; FICA - when no longer subject to substantial risk of forfeiture. For Employer: No deduction when promised, deduction when employee includes in income.

sections.title: Summary
items: NQDC = unsecured promise to pay; executive is a general creditor
Section 409A: Election timing, 6 distribution events, severe penalties
409A violation: Immediate income + 20% penalty + interest from deferral year
Rabbi trust: Protects against management changes, NOT insolvency
FICA taxed when vested; income tax when distributed

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is a key difference between a qualified retirement plan and a non-qualified deferred compensation (NQDC) plan?
options: Qualified plans generally do not allow for elective deferrals, while NQDC plans always do.
Qualified plans are subject to ERISA and offer tax advantages, while NQDC plans may not be ERISA-covered and defer taxes without immediate taxation.
Qualified plans are primarily for highly compensated employees, while NQDC plans are for all employees.
Qualified plans are funded with after-tax dollars, while NQDC plans are funded with pre-tax dollars.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Qualified plans are subject to ERISA, and offer tax advantages like immediate deduction for contributions and tax-deferred growth. NQDC plans may or may not be subject to ERISA (depending on design, e.g., Top Hat plans). They defer taxes until distribution, but contributions are not immediately deductible by the employee. The employer receives a deduction when the employee recognizes the income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the ERISA implications and the timing of tax deductions for both the employee and the employer.

reveal.question: What are the key requirements under Section 409A that must be followed to avoid immediate taxation and penalties on NQDC?
reveal.answer: Section 409A requires that NQDC plans have a fixed deferral election, restrict distributions to specific permissible events (separation from service, disability, death, a specified time, change in control, or unforeseeable emergency), and prohibit acceleration of distributions except under very limited circumstances. Initial deferral elections generally must be made in the year preceding the year of service. Subsequent deferral elections are generally allowed, but must be made at least 12 months before the first scheduled payment.

rows.label: Employee Eligibility
values: May include a wider range of employees, although still generally management or highly compensated
Strictly limited to a select group of management or highly compensated employees

rows.label: ERISA Coverage
values: May be subject to some ERISA requirements (e.g., reporting and disclosure), depending on plan design
Exempt from most ERISA provisions, including participation, vesting, funding, and fiduciary requirements

rows.label: Funding
values: May be informally funded (e.g., with corporate owned life insurance) or unfunded
Typically unfunded, relying on the employer's general assets

title: Stock Options and Equity Compensation
description: Compare incentive stock options (ISOs) to non-qualified options (NQSOs)
topics: Compare incentive stock options (ISOs) to non-qualified options (NQSOs)
Calculate tax consequences of stock option exercises
Explain restricted stock and RSU taxation

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Equity compensation aligns employee interests with shareholders and can create significant wealth—but with complex tax considerations.

sections.title: Two Types of Stock Options
rows: 2 years from grant AND 1 year from exercise

sections.title: NQSO Tax Treatment
sections.content: At Exercise: Spread (FMV - Exercise Price) = ordinary income, added to W-2. At Sale: Capital gain/loss = Sale Price - FMV at exercise. Holding Period: ≤1 year = STCG, >1 year = LTCG.

sections.title: NQSO Example
sections.content: Grant at $10 exercise price. Exercise when FMV = $50. Ordinary income = $40/share. Sell at $70. Capital gain = $20/share (from $50 FMV at exercise).

sections.title: ISO Tax Treatment - Qualified
sections.content: At Exercise: No regular income tax (but spread is AMT preference item). At Sale (if qualified): All gain is LTCG from exercise price. Holding requirements: 2+ years from grant date AND 1+ year from exercise date.

sections.title: Exam Tip
sections.content: ISO Holding Period: 2-1 (2 years from grant, 1 year from exercise). Disqualifying disposition (sold before holding periods met) = spread at exercise becomes ordinary income.

sections.title: $100,000 ISO Limit
sections.content: ISOs that first become exercisable in any year cannot exceed $100,000 in value (based on grant date price). Excess is treated as NQSOs.

sections.title: AMT Planning for ISOs
sections.content: Problem: Exercising ISOs creates AMT preference item equal to the spread—can trigger massive AMT without cash to pay. Strategies: Spread exercises over years, same-year sale (disqualifying), model AMT impact, exercise at low spread.

sections.title: Restricted Stock and RSUs
rows: Capital gain from FMV at vest
Capital gain from FMV at vest

sections.title: Section 83(b) Election
sections.content: For restricted stock only (not RSUs). Elect to pay tax at grant instead of vesting. Pay tax on lower value, future appreciation = capital gain. Must file within 30 days of grant. Risk: Forfeit shares = no tax refund.

sections.title: Summary
items: NQSOs: Spread is ordinary income at exercise; employer deduction
ISOs: No regular tax at exercise (AMT preference); LTCG if qualified
ISO holding: 2 years from grant + 1 year from exercise for LTCG
83(b) election for restricted stock: Tax at grant, file within 30 days
RSUs: Taxed at vesting as ordinary income; no 83(b) election

knowledgeCheck.question: An employee is granted NQSOs with an exercise price of $20 per share. When the fair market value (FMV) of the stock is $60 per share, the employee exercises the options for 100 shares. What is the amount of ordinary income the employee will recognize at the time of exercise?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The ordinary income recognized at exercise is the difference between the FMV and the exercise price, multiplied by the number of shares. ($60 - $20) * 100 = $4,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that with NQSOs, the bargain element (FMV - exercise price) is taxed as ordinary income at the time of exercise.

reveal.question: How is the holding period determined for shares acquired through the exercise of Non-Qualified Stock Options (NQSOs)?
reveal.answer: The holding period for shares acquired through the exercise of NQSOs begins on the day *after* the options are exercised.

rows.label: Sale
values: Capital Gain/Loss (Sale Price - FMV at Exercise)
STCG (<= 1 year holding), LTCG (> 1 year holding)

title: Retirement Planning for Business Owners
description: Compare retirement plan options for business owners
topics: Compare retirement plan options for business owners
Design plans to maximize owner vs. employee contributions
Apply plan selection criteria based on business goals

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Business owners have unique retirement planning opportunities—and challenges—compared to employees.

sections.title: Plan Selection Framework
rows: Maximize for owner or include employees?

sections.title: Solo 401(k) Deep Dive
sections.content: For self-employed with no employees (except spouse). Employee deferral: $24,500 ($32,000 if 50+). Employer contribution: 20% of net SE income. Total: $71,500 ($79,000 if 50+). Advantages: Roth option available, loans permitted, can contribute more than SEP at lower incomes.

sections.title: Key Insight
sections.content: Solo 401(k) is almost always better for solo practitioners with income under ~$360,000 because of the employee deferral component.

sections.title: Defined Benefit Plans for Owners
sections.content: When DB plans shine: Age 50+, high stable income, want large deductions, few/no employees. Maximum benefit (2026): $290,000/year lifetime annuity at 62 OR 100% of 3-year average. Contribution determined by actuary - older owner = larger contribution needed.

sections.title: Cash Balance Plans
sections.content: Hybrid approach: Legally a defined benefit plan, looks like defined contribution (individual accounts). Pay credit (% of salary) + Interest credit (guaranteed return). Higher contributions than 401(k) limits, can be combined with 401(k).

sections.title: Owner Strategy by Age
rows: Solo 401(k) or SEP - time is the advantage
Solo 401(k) for most; consider DB add-on if high income
Defined Benefit becomes powerful; larger contributions allowed

sections.title: Summary
items: Solo 401(k) usually beats SEP for self-employed under $345K income
Defined benefit allows largest contributions, especially for older owners
Cash balance plans = hybrid with DB contribution levels, DC appearance
Must cover eligible employees at similar contribution rates
Combine DB + 401(k) for maximum contributions and flexibility

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, age 42 and self-employed, operates a consulting business as a sole proprietor. Her net self-employment income after business expenses is $100,000. What is the MAXIMUM total (employee + employer) contribution she can make to a Solo 401(k) for the current year, assuming the employee deferral limit is $24,500 and the total contribution limit is $71,500?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah can defer $24,500 as the employee. As the employer, she can contribute 20% of her net self-employment income, which is 0.20 * $100,000 = $20,000. Her total contribution is $24,500 + $20,000 = $44,500. While the total contribution limit is $71,500, she is limited by her income and the 20% employer contribution rule.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate both the employee deferral and the employer contribution. The employer contribution is capped at 20% of net self-employment income.

reveal.question: Name three advantages of a Solo 401(k) compared to a SEP IRA for a self-employed individual.
reveal.answer: 1. Higher potential contribution limits, especially at lower incomes. 2. The option to make Roth contributions. 3. The ability to take loans from the plan.

sections.title: Solo 401(k) Contribution as Employee vs. Employer

rows.label: Contribution Type
values: Profit-sharing contribution based on net self-employment income

title: Retirement Plan Distribution Strategies
description: Apply RMD rules across account types
topics: Apply RMD rules across account types

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: How and when you take distributions can significantly impact after-tax retirement income.

sections.title: RMD Aggregation Rules
items: Traditional IRAs: Calculate separately, aggregate, satisfy from ANY Traditional IRA
401(k) Plans: Each plan's RMD must be taken from that plan
403(b) Plans: Can aggregate like IRAs

sections.title: Tax-Efficient Distribution Sequencing
sections.content: Goal: Minimize lifetime taxes, not just current-year taxes. Common ordering (taxable → tax-deferred → tax-free) may not be optimal. Issues: Wasted low brackets early, large RMDs later, IRMAA surcharges, Social Security taxation. Better approach: Fill tax brackets strategically, consider Roth conversions during low-income years.

sections.title: Roth Conversion Strategies
rows: Convert more shares for same tax

sections.title: Exam Tip
sections.content: Know the 2026 tax bracket boundaries for MFJ: 10% up to $24,500, 12% up to $99,550, 22% up to $212,300, 24% up to $405,400. Fill lower brackets with taxable income; use Roth for overflow.

sections.title: 72(t) SEPP Requirements
rows: At least 5 years OR until age 59½ (whichever is longer)

sections.title: 72(t) Example
sections.content: IRA Balance: $500,000, Age: 50, Life expectancy: 34.2 years. Life Expectancy Method: Year 1 = $500,000 / 34.2 = $14,620/year. Amortization (at 5%): ~$30,000/year. Warning: Locked in for 5+ years or until 59½; modification = retroactive penalty on all withdrawals.

sections.title: QCD Benefits
sections.content: Not included in income (unlike regular distribution), satisfies RMD, benefits even if taking standard deduction. For those who don't itemize, QCD is effectively better than taking distribution and donating.

sections.title: Summary
items: RMD starts at 73 (75 for 1960+); first year can delay to April 1 following
IRAs aggregate for RMDs (distribute from any); 401(k)s do not aggregate
Roth conversions: Fill low brackets in early retirement before SS/RMDs
72(t) SEPP: 5 years or until 59½ (longer); modification = retroactive penalty
QCD at 70½+: Up to $105K excluded from income; counts toward RMD

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs)?
options: RMDs must be taken from each individual IRA account, calculating the RMD separately for each.
RMDs are not required for Roth IRAs during the owner's lifetime.
RMDs for 403(b) accounts can be satisfied by distributions from a Roth 401(k) account.
RMDs can be satisfied by taking distributions from a taxable brokerage account.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: RMDs are not required from Roth IRAs during the owner's lifetime. RMDs are calculated across all traditional IRAs. 403(b) RMDs cannot be satisfied by a Roth 401(k). RMDs must come from qualified accounts.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which accounts are subject to income tax.

reveal.question: What conditions trigger a penalty if a 72(t) distribution plan is modified before age 59 1/2 or 5 years, whichever is later?
reveal.answer: Modification (other than death or disability) triggers retroactive penalties on all payments received prior to the modification, plus interest.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following distribution sequences is MOST likely to minimize lifetime taxes for a client in retirement, assuming stable tax rates and a moderate income?
options: A strategic mix of taxable, tax-deferred, and tax-free to fill lower tax brackets and manage IRMAA surcharges.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A strategic approach that fills lower tax brackets and manages potential issues like IRMAA surcharges is generally the most tax-efficient. The traditional order (taxable -> tax-deferred -> tax-free) often leads to higher RMDs and higher overall taxes.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the impact of RMDs and Medicare surcharges on the long-term tax picture.
